data.title: Artesian Resources: A Growing Customer Base Drives Growth
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

When was the last time that you turned on the faucet in your house and no water came out? What did you do when that happened, if you can remember a time of that occurring?

We take for granted the utilities around us because we expect them to work. Regulated utilities are overwhelmingly regulated monopolies. You don't get to choose what water company or power company you have in most states. While some states will allow you to have the choice of power company, like Texas, you rarely get the opportunity to pick what water utility you are connected to. They own the pipes to your house and the pipes away from your house, and unless you're on a septic tank, you depend on them for both fresh water and wastewater.

The way most of these utilities grow is either through customer base growth or pricing increases. Both of these happen in an expected, regular way across the United States; you can benefit tremendously from the clear and expected cash flows from these companies. I love utilities in my portfolio because they provide a steadily growing dividend base that I can use to invest in other opportunities that I like as well.

When I'm building a fortress of a portfolio, I want fortress-strength companies in there. I'm not building my castle of dividend-paying companies out of chaff and weeds. I'm building them out of bricks and stones, companies that are stable, strong, and growing.

Let's look at one of these companies today!

A Water Utility To Tap Into

As of Sept. 30, 2025, Artesian Resources (ARTNA), a regulated water and wastewater utility, served 98,800 water customers in all three counties of Delaware through its Artesian Water subsidiary. It also served 2,700 customers in portions of Maryland through its Artesian Water Maryland subsidiary and 40 customers in Pennsylvania through its Artesian Water Pennsylvania subsidiary. On the wastewater side, Artesian Wastewater and TESI provided wastewater services to 9,000 customers in Delaware.

The investment thesis for ARTNA is essentially unchanged from when Kody last covered it with a "Buy" rating a few weeks ago. The regulated utility continues to invest in upgrading and expanding its infrastructure to accommodate customer growth. In the first nine months of 2025, ARTNA invested $40.5 million in water and wastewater infrastructure – a huge growth rate over $30.9 million in YTD 2024. These funds were allocated to install new mains, upgrade and replace meter reading equipment, rehab aging infrastructure, and upgrade existing pumping and treatment stations.

ARTNA hasn't just been investing capital without seeking a fair and just return, either. In April, the company filed with Delaware's Public Service Commission to receive a 10.75% net incremental increase in its revenue. That would represent a $9.4 million boost to the top line. This remains on track to likely be approved at some point this calendar year, with my hunch being that most of the increase will be allowed. That's because it properly allows ARTNA to recover its higher electric supply expenses and recent investments.

This is why I believe that the sole FAST Graphs analyst estimate for diluted EPS is sensible. In 2025, diluted EPS is expected to grow by 10.1% to $2.18. In 2026, higher electric supply expenses and elevated investments are expected to lead to an 8.3% decline in diluted EPS to $2. For 2027, it's predicted that diluted EPS will climb 10% to $2.20. Beyond that time, I think mid-single-digit annual diluted EPS growth is attainable.

Financially, ARTNA has the means to invest in business growth. As of Sept. 30, 2025, the company had a total of $59.7 million of undrawn lines of credit with Citizens Bank ($39.7 million of $40 million) and CoBank (completely undrawn for $20 million).

ARTNA's 4% dividend yield is appealing and sustainable. For 2026, the diluted EPS payout ratio is positioned to be in the mid-60% range. That provides it with breathing room in what will likely be a temporary down year for diluted EPS. This is why I believe that ARTNA can extend the 29-year dividend growth streak that makes it a Dividend Champion. ARTNA's 5-year CAGR of its dividend is 4.1%. This gives it an 8% Chowder Number, right at the attractive threshold for a utility.

ARTNA's value proposition is arguably attractive. At the current $32 share price, the water utility is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 15.8 (100% weight for 2026). This clocks in far under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 23.2, per FAST Graphs.

Looking ahead, I would argue that a valuation multiple of 18.5 is a sensible fair value estimate. That considers that ARTNA's long-term growth prospects are holding up and that higher interest rates are likely to meaningfully pressure its valuation (relative to the past decade) in the years ahead. This equates to a 15% discount to fair value.

If ARTNA reverts to fair value and lives up to growth expectations, it could log a 21% upside in 2026. Through 2030, the water utility could be set up for 11% annual total returns.

ARTNA does face risks that could alter the investment thesis, though. The chief risk for the water utility is undoubtedly its geographic concentration in Delaware. As Kody explained in September, 97.3% of ARTNA's 101,540 water customers resided in Delaware. The entirety of its 9,000 wastewater customers lived in Delaware, too.

That means one must be confident that Delaware's Public Service Commission will continue to be fair with its rate case decisions. If ARTNA is handed down adverse rate case decisions in the future, that could crimp its profit margins. This could, in turn, put pressure on the stock's sentiment, which could hurt shareholder returns.

Delaware is also ranked 20th in the nation in terms of the number of FEMA disaster declarations. Especially severe natural disasters could disrupt ARTNA's operations, which could weigh on its financial results.

Conclusion

ARTNA is an attractive water utility that is positioned in the United States in a location that is expected to see continued population growth. This allows them, along with their capital spending plan, to grow their revenue and thus grow the money they can pay to you and the value of their shares as a whole.

Being a dividend investor does not mean that you should forego a high level of total return in any way, shape, or form. We expect to receive our total return as a combination of both the dividends received and the change in price. That is the basics of the total return equation, and we're able to capitalize on a high total return by buying undervalued opportunities, allowing us to enjoy the reversion back to mean - to a historical PE ratio - as well as the dividends along the way.

Being a Dividend King means investing with clarity and being able to see through all the confusion and noise that's in the market or the news cycle. You can find great clarity and financial freedom by owning a portfolio of fortress-quality investments that pay you regular dividends.

data.title: Krystal Biotech: Beyond VYJUVEK Commercialization
data.description:
Last November, I reiterated my bullish stance on Krystal Biotech (NASDAQ:KRYS) as commercialization of their topical gene therapy Vyjuvek expanded internationally in Europe and Japan. Vyjuvek treats Dystrophic Epidermolysis Bullosa (DEB), a serious rare genetic disease caused by mutations of the COL7A1 gene. In only 2 months, Krystal's stock has rallied from $205 to $285, a robust 39% gain.

Analysts have taken note of the successful Vyjuvek commercialization, forecasting 45% revenue growth to $564M in 2026. If correct, this indicates that the stock trades for approximately 35.5x 2026 earnings and 16.5x 2026 sales. Management has blockbuster aspirations for Vyjuvek, targeting over $1B in sales over the coming years. However, Krystal's share price has now discounted the majority of Vyjuvek's growth, as doubling sales to blockbuster status would represent an above-average multiple in the biotech sub-industry of ~8x. Perhaps this is reasonable for a company that demonstrated fantastic gross margins of 96% (2025-Q3-10-Q: page 20) and ten consecutive quarters of positive EPS. With nearly $1B in cash and investments as of Q4 2025 (JPM Presentation: Slide 4), Krystal has a fortress balance sheet to fuel growth. However, investors must now move on to the question: what catalysts will achieve this future growth?

As a platform gene delivery company, Krystal aspires beyond a single blockbuster drug and continues to execute on its pipeline. Last week, Phase 1 data were reported from CORAL-1, a dose-escalation study that has demonstrated inhaled delivery of the CFTR gene via the company's KB407 clinical candidate for cystic fibrosis (CF). Another major development was Krystal's presentation at the J.P. Morgan healthcare conference last week.

The angle that investors may not yet realize is that 2026 is a data year for Krystal, with the following key catalysts:

  • KB803: Phase 3 readout in corneal abrasions in DEB patients.
  • KB801: Registrational readout in neurotrophic keratitis (NK).
  • KB407: Initiation and full enrollment of the registrational repeat-dosing study in CF.
  • KB111: Initiation and full enrollment of the registrational repeat-dosing study in Hailey-Hailey Disease (HHD).
  • KB408 and KB707: Pipeline updates in the inhaled delivery Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency (AATD) and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) programs.

The recent success for inhaled delivery substantially derisks several of the coming catalysts. Prior to the Phase 1 data, inhaled delivery could have been viewed as somewhat speculative, with no previous successful examples. Both KB407, KB408, and KB707 rely on the same Aerogen Solo® Nebulizer System and share the same HSV-1 viral vector. Therefore, success in CF demonstrates that the viral vector can withstand delivery, successfully delivering genetic material expressed in the lungs, directly derisking upcoming readouts for AATD and NSCLC.

Thus, Krystal's platform provides a high probability that the early success of Vyjuvek is not a one-time event; rather, it is a strategy that can be executed in serial format to discover subsequent blockbuster drugs. Given the high concentration of catalysts in the coming 12 months, I upgrade Krystal to a Strong Buy recommendation, even in light of the higher share price.

Krystal Biotech: J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference Update

Krystal Biotech presented this week at the 44th Annual J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference, underscoring the company's foundational growth with its first drug, Vyjuvek. Vyjuvek is the first redosable drug in genetic medicine, a topical gel that could be conveniently dosed at home, providing relief to patients suffering from DEB. Following FDA approval in 2023, the product launch has been a huge success in the past 2 years. With new expansion in Europe and Japan, strong growth is anticipated in the coming years.

By 2030, Krystal anticipates the launch of up to four rare disease medicines in DEB (Vyjuvek and KB803), HHD (KB111), CF (KB407), and NK (KB801). In total, these diseases present a patient population of approximately 10,000, roughly quadruple the patient population addressed by Vyjuvek (~2500).

KB803 is an eye drop formulation that treats lesions in the eyes of DEB patients, with a prevalence of ~1,000-plus in Europe and the US. The market potential is estimated at around $300M, and the registration-enabling study IOLITE has a primary completion date in March 2026. This is a near-term revenue growth catalyst for Krystal.

The second commercialization catalyst is KB801 in NK with a ~$1B market potential. KB801 is also an eye drop formulation potentially used to treat NK, a rare, degenerative corneal disease. In July 2025, the first patient was dosed in EMERALD-1 in July 2025, with a primary completion date estimated around October 2026. The market potential is estimated based on sales of Oxervate, which topped $1B last year. Oxervate is a recombinant human nerve growth factor that must be dosed 6x daily for eight weeks, and Krystal presented target engagement data in mice at JPM of NGF levels post-treatment that were significantly higher. Thus, Krystal believes KB801 can achieve a dominant market position in NK.

The case for KB407 will be discussed in the next section, along with new Phase 1 data in CF. Lastly, KB111 for HHD is phenotypically very similar to DEB. While it is a different gene that is delivered (ATP2Cl), the same novel vector also treats patients with skin lesions. The $1B market potential is based upon Krystal's estimate of 10-15k patients who suffer from HHD in Europe and the US. The timeline is similar to KB407, with a registrational start in 2026, potentially leading to a BLA submission in 2027, potentially supporting a commercial launch in 2028.

Thus, Krystal aims to transition from Vyjuvek to a total of four drugs in DEB, NK, CF, and HHD by the end of the decade. This potentially positions the company to capture sales in the ~$5B range should they prove successful. This is the upside case that could continue to provide investors with substantial returns in the coming years.

Krystal Biotech: Recent KB407 Data in CF

On January 8th, Krystal announced a positive interim clinical update from its Phase 1 CORAL-1 study evaluating KB407 in patients with CF.

A total of seven patients were dosed, of whom four were modulator ineligible. These are patients with specific genetic mutations who do not respond to currently available treatments, such as Vertex's (NASDAQ:VRTX) Trikafta. Within this small cohort, the percentage of conducting airway cells positive for CFTR or viral marker varied between an overall range of 29.4% and 42.1%.

These results confirm the successful lung delivery and expression of wild-type CFTR protein following inhaled administration of KB407. The data give Krystal conviction to move rapidly into larger studies with repeat dosing

Krystal is moving to begin its registrational study (CORAL-3), with full enrollment expected this year. This commercial catalyst is further out, but following full enrollment data will be analyzed, potentially leading to a Biologics License Application (BLA) to the FDA in 2027 and commercialization in 2028.

This is a huge market potential ($2B+). While drugs, such as Vertex's Trikafta, address the majority of CF patients, a significant (~10-15%) minority are modulator ineligible. These patients do not respond to current drugs and represent approximately 30,000 total global patients. In these patients, the body stops producing CFTR altogether.

Vertex's Trikafta achieved $8.9B in 2023 sales; thus, 10-15% in a smaller population is anticipated to be a $2B opportunity since the drug's price will increase within the smaller cohort. While we cannot be certain that KB407 will prove successful in CF, the strong CFTR expression data bode well for success in larger studies with repeat dosing.

In the coming year, market anticipation of a fully subscribed pivotal study will provide a catalyst for the stock. In addition, KB408 for AATD and KB707 for NSCLC both have important data coming in 2026. Because they use the same inhaled delivery method, these catalysts are effectively derisked and could present investors with further upside.

Krystal Biotech: Analyst Expectations for Growth

While analysts have largely priced in the rapid increase in Vyjuvek's sales, they are not as yet seeing the value in the company's pipeline. Vyjuvek reached $107M in Q4 sales as pre-released at JPM, an annual run rate of ~$425M. Analysts see this run rate continuing to grow by 32.7% in 2026 as the commercial launch proceeds in Japan and Europe.

However, the two commercialization catalysts set to arrive in 2026 (KB801 & KB803) are set to embark Krystal on new commercialization campaigns, potentially leading to $1.3B in annual sales over time. This setup still seems likely to leave Krystal room to surpass analysts estimates if the Vyjuvek launch continues to be strong.

With >90% gross margins, this positions Krystal to compound its earnings at a stellar rate. Analysts predicting Vyjuvek blockbuster sales ($1.15B in 2029) predict $20/share in EPS at that time. Should sales growth be in the high range anticipated above, the operating leverage of the company will be on full display.

In the past 12 months, SG&A expenses were $136.6M, while R&D expenses were $56.8M; thus total expenses were $193.4M. The cost to manufacture Vyjuvek was only $21.4M, leaving an operating income of $158.3M from total revenue of $373.2M (operating margin = 42.4%).

But the beautiful part of Krystal's business model is that if growth can be as robust as projections, the SG&A and R&D expenses will not rise at the same rate as revenues. This is why, as analysts see sales reaching a $1.15B Vyjuvek blockbuster scenario, they anticipate $20/share in EPS in 2029. In the table below, I use less optimistic assumptions but recognize that SG&A and R&D expenses will not scale up to match revenues in an optimistic bull case.

Metric* Vyjuvek Blockbuster Vyjuvek + Eye Franchise Full Lung/Eye Portfolio
Annual Sales $1150M $3000M $5000M
SG&A Expenses $345M $750M $1250M
R&D Expenses + Misc $200M $350M $600M
Est. Operating Income $605M $1900M $3350M
Operating Margin 52.6% 63.3% 67.0%
Est. Net Income (21% tax rate) $478M $1501M $2647M
Shares Outstanding 29M 29M 29M
Projected EPS $16.48 $51.76 $91.28
Implied Share Price (20x P/E) $329.66 $1035.17 $1825.52

*Author's hypothetical growth projections - Krystal in 2031

In the first case, blockbuster peak sales of Vyjuvek lead to sales of $1.15B; this seems close to what the market is presently discounting. A middle case envisions a scenario where Krystal sees strong growth, but less than the fully bullish $5B in sales outlined at JPM. Perhaps CF, NK, or HHD are only moderately successful, or some fail.

However, a fully bullish scenario ($5B in sales) will position Krystal to expand its operating margin considerably. At 20x projected EPS of $91.28/share, this would lead to a share price of over $1800 by 2030. Krystal is executing superbly, and management's forecasts could position the stock as what Peter Lynch would call a 10-bagger. I first covered Krystal when the share price was $185/share and now anticipate that this could be my second 10-bagger stock on Seeking Alpha (my first was Tesla, which I rated a buy at $12.39/share).

Conclusion

Krystal Biotech has successfully navigated the transition from clinical hope to commercial reality. Vyjuvek currently generates a current run rate of approximately $425M in annual sales, placing Krystal on firm financial footing with nearly $1B to fund growth. Now the investment thesis turns to the scalability of Krystal's platform to deliver subsequent gene therapies.

The recent data in CF for KB407 and additional catalysts in 2026 provide promising catalysts for the stock in the coming year. Major Phase 3 readouts and pivotal data are coming in ocular DEB, CF, and NK; and inhaled delivery promises to build on Phase 1 success in CF in AATD and NSCLC. As such, Krystal Biotech presents a compelling Strong Buy at current prices.

data.title: Boston Scientific: Adding Another Growth Engine With Penumbra
data.description:
Towards the end of October, I concluded that Boston Scientific (BSX) was continuing its M&A playbook, just as organic achievements were impressive. Supported by nearly 20% organic sales growth, shares commanded premium earnings valuations in the low thirties, and while this was demanding, as impressive was the organic growth trajectory.

This organic growth remains the same, but as Boston Scientific starts the year with a huge and nearly $15 billion deal for Penumbra (PEN), the story does now involve leverage. This seems manageable, yet it adds another near-term dilutive growth pillar into 2026, albeit at slightly lower share price levels.

Turning It into A Growth Engine

Boston describes itself as a growth company being conditioned for high performance, as the company has successfully combined organic growth with superior products and a fairly aggressive M&A strategy.

In 2024, the company generated nearly $17 billion in sales, with about two thirds of these sales tied to the cardiovascular group, in its turn consisting out of electrophysiology, cardiac rhythm management, WATCHMAN, interventional cardiology therapies, and peripheral interventions. The remaining third in sales is the medsurg segment, focusing on endoscopy, urology, and neuromodulation.

In the 2010s, Boston Scientific has been somewhat of a struggling scientific and medical device maker, as revenues have only risen from about $7 billion to $10 billion over this period of time. This came as the company had seen a real overhang from a $27 billion deal from Guidant in the late 2000s, creating a real overhang on the performance of the stock in the early 2010s.

After reshifting the focus of the business and returning to growth in the 2010s, the company was setting up for greater growth, as sales essentially doubled between 2020 and 2025, although that M&A played a role in all this.

The Base Performance

Early in 2025, the company reported truly impressive results for the year 2024. Revenues rose by no less than 18% to $16.7 billion, with growth driven by the larger cardiovascular sales group. Adjusted earnings rose some forty-six cents to $2.51 per share, although GAAP earnings only came in at half that level. While amortization and intangible charges played a key role in this, there were some cash charges involved as well.

With Boston guiding for 2025 organic sales to rise by another 10-12%, with reported sales growth seen around 2.5% higher, earnings were seen moving up to $2.80-$2.87 per share. Trading around the $100 mark, valuations were rather demanding, with shares trading at 40 times trailing adjusted earnings, even as the forward multiple dropped to the low thirties.

Following some bolt-on M&A deals that have occurred this year, the company has been hiking the guidance alongside each quarterly earnings report released through 2025. Third quarter reported sales rose by 20%, with organic growth reported around 15%. The adjusted earnings guidance was hiked to a midpoint of $3.03 per share. Trading around the $100 mark at the time, shares traded at 33-34 times earnings, these furthermore being adjusted earnings.

This was driven by impressive 15% organic growth, which in its turn was driven by nearly 35% organic growth at Watchman and nearly 63% organic growth in electrophysiology.

On top of the prevailing $154 billion equity valuation, Boston operated a net debt load of $11 billion, for around a 2 times leverage ratio. A resulting $165 billion enterprise valuation translates into an 8 times sales multiple, and of course, a low-thirty times earnings multiple, albeit that 15% organic growth given the size of the business was rather impressive. None of that was about to change as the company announced a bolt-on $533 million cash deal to acquire a remaining majority ownership stake in Nalu Medical.

I felt uneasy chasing the shares but was appreciative of the organic growth as well. Forwarding about a quarter in time, shares have seen a roughly 10% setback, now trading around the $90 mark.

Starting 2026 With More M&A

In the second week of the year, the company announced a bolt-on deal for Valencia Technologies, as no financial details on the purchase price or contribution of the business were communicated, besides that the transaction was immaterial.

The much larger deal took place a few days later as the company announced the acquisition of Penumbra in a cash and stock deal valued at $374 per share, valuing the enterprise at around $14.5 billion. Investors can either elect for $374 per share in cash or 3.8721 shares of Boston Scientific, valued near $349 per share based on a $90 stock price of Boston.

However, the total allocation is fixed between 73% in cash and 27% in stock, implying a roughly $11 billion cash payment and $4 billion stock issuance, indicating about 41 million shares to be issued. Frankly, this looks like a good deal for investors in Penumbra, being awarded a big premium at around 10 times sales and 100 times the non-GAAP earnings here.

The acquisition results in the addition of novel technologies in the vessel space used to treat conditions such as pulmonary embolism, stroke, deep vein thrombosis, acute limb ischemia, heart attacks, among others.

The purchase does not come cheap, with Penumbra seeing full-year sales up just over 17% to $1.4 billion, resulting in a 10 times sales multiple. However, Penumbra saw fourth quarter sales up as much as 21-22%, marking a nice acceleration of growth. The deal is expected to be dilutive by 6-8 cents in the first year upon closing, neutral the year thereafter, and accretive from there onwards.

What Now?

The deal for Penumbra is a bit bigger, as shares sold off by nearly 4%, or $4 per share, on the deal announcement. With over 1.5 billion shares outstanding, including the shares to be issued in connection to the deal, some $6 billion in value has gone up into some in reaction to a substantial $145 billion deal, and this feels like an overreaction.

Net debt will double to $22 billion, and while this increase is substantial, I am not too worried about it. This comes amidst the degree of profits and predictable growth displayed by Boston Scientific. Hence, I am not too worried about it, although that leverage is not low with the own business posting trailing EBITDA at $5.8 billion as of the third quarter.

That results in a 3.8 times leverage ratio based on these numbers, with the EBITDA contribution from Penumbra likely resulting in a mid-3 times leverage ratio, given that I peg Penumbra's EBITDA at around a quarter of a billion.

Investors are likely to be more worried about the higher sales multiple paid, the degree of leverage taken on, and the dilution to near-term earnings. Investors are skeptical, nonetheless, driven by the relatively higher multiple paid, as Boston itself is not the cheapest, as the acquired activities have lower margins as well. Given all this and the debt taken on, investors are cautious, but then again, Boston Scientific has been a stronger acquirer recently with quite a reputation, although this is a larger deal.

With earnings power for 2025 seen around $3 per share and some modest dilution seen from the deal, I still see earnings well north of $3 per share in 2026, driven by the continuation of growth. Hence, a $90 stock commands a 30 times earnings multiple, or just below that, but now with quite some debt incurred.

Given all this, I am slightly more appealed to the shares given the 10% setback, as Penumbra looks like a fine business, though it does not come cheap. Within the Boston operations, more potential seems to be here, also for both cost and revenue synergies.

Given all this, I am keen to keep a closer eye on shares of Boston Scientific into 2026 here, yet I am looking for a continuation of organic growth and some further pullback to start seeing appeal here.

data.title: Spotify: Buy The Dip As $12.99 Price Takes Hold
data.description:
So far in early 2026, the market is showing visible signs of nervousness. Stocks are trading choppily to start the year, particularly the tech gainers that drove much of the market's rally last year. Investors are carefully watching weaker macroeconomic signals, plus rising geopolitical tensions and the possibility of an escalation of the White House's campaign against an independent Fed.

Against this backdrop, I continue to emphasize individual stock selection, particularly among recent decliners that have room to rebound. Spotify Technology S.A. (SPOT) is an excellent choice here: the leading music streaming company has been in a relentless downtrend since June (when the stock briefly touched $785), and has lost ~35% of its value since then, technically putting Spotify in a deep bear market. Despite this, fundamentals continue to hum along quite nicely.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Spotify in November, when the stock was trading at $640 per share. While I acknowledge that the timing of my buy call was premature, my position remains the same. Spotify is what I consider to be an "evergreen" company. While there is certainly competition in music streaming (Apple Music, most notably, and Amazon secondarily), there are few competitors that match Spotify's scale, and its subscriber base has shown tremendous loyalty toward the platform. I reiterate my buy rating here.

Another price increase is baked in for 2026

First, let's address the more recent news that Spotify has printed. In late November, after the company's most recent fiscal Q3 earnings release, Spotify announced that it was raising prices yet again in its largest core market, the U.S. For existing subscribers, the company will start to bill $12.99/month (up 8% from the current $11.99/month) for the billing date that lands in February.

The chart above now showcases the company's pricing for each plan type, following the price change. We do note that this price move is a bit of a gamble on Spotify's part. The company already sits $1/month above Apple Music prices (for individual plans) and substantially more for family plans. The comparable Apple Music U.S. prices are shown below:

We'll address the competitive dynamics shortly, but what investors should be aware of here is that from a pure comps perspective, Spotify stands to gain from a y/y perspective. In FY24, the company raised U.S. prices from $10.99/month to $11.99/month (a 9% increase) in July 2024.

This means that FY25 had a half-year headwind from lapping the price increase. While Spotify doesn't report revenue by geo, we do note that revenue growth decelerated from 22% y/y growth in Q1'25 and 17% growth in Q2'25, to 16% growth in Q3'25 (when the increase was fully lapped).

Conversely, when we look ahead to FY26 (which is what is driving the stock now), we expect tailwinds from price moves. The $12.99 price increase that went into effect in February will essentially be a benefit for nearly the full year, while Spotify will not be competing against any price increases for FY25.

The same is true for European markets. Now, running these comparisons in Europe is a bit trickier since price changes varied slightly by market. But in general, pricing in Europe went up from €10.99 to €11.99 in the August through Novembertimeframe for many key markets. Meanwhile, the last time Europe had a price increase (from €9.99 to €10.99 was in late 2023). So in Europe, FY25 contained only ~1 quarter of pricing benefit, while FY26 will benefit from three quarters of the €11.99 price point before lapping the increases in the late Q3/early Q4 timeframe.

Premium feature differentiation will help retention

So, in short, from a pure pricing comparable perspective, Spotify should see tailwinds in FY26. Of course, the key assumption here that is a major unknown is what will happen with subscriber retention, especially in a tough macroeconomy.

What we have observed so far, however, is that price increases have had a minor dent on user trends. The chart below showcases Spotify's user trends by quarter. Look in particular back to the second half of FY25, when the $10.99 to $11.99 pricing round kicked off in the U.S. (of note is the fact that this is the first time the company's pricing materially diverged from Apple). The company still added Premium subscribers on a sequential basis. (Premium subs of 252 million in Q3'24 increased by 6 million sequentially from 246 million subs in Q2'24, not shown in the chart below).

These are all core indications that price increases are a net revenue driver with low elasticity for Spotify. Also helping the situation is the fact that Spotify is now more clearly distinguishing the benefits of Premium versus ad-supported beyond simply removing ads.

In December, the company announced it was rolling out music video content exclusively for Premium subscribers, building on the popularity of short-form videos on social media platforms. This additional feature, plus the fact of playlists that are locked into Spotify (and make switching to a competitor like Apple Music very difficult), is a positive tailwind for retention.

Q4 look-ahead, valuation, key takeaways

Spotify is expected to report Q4 results in early February. For me, the key watch list items are:

  • Premium subscriber trends and European user retention. Q4 will be the first full (or near-full) quarter of €11.99 pricing in Europe, and we're keen to see the Premium subscriber results to see if any meaningful changes in retention occurred.
  • Anecdotal U.S. churn feedback. The company will be reporting earnings right around the time that it's raising prices to $12.99 in the U.S., but the increase is already live for brand-new subs. We should also keep a lookout for management's commentary on how U.S. user trends shape out after this price increase, especially given the wider gap to Apple Music pricing.
  • Ad revenue trends amid a choppy economy. More recently, ad revenue growth (another core monetization angle for Spotify that it's been pushing) has been decelerating under the weight of a weaker macro. However, many major owners of ad inventory (like Meta) have seen acceleration in advertising revenue. We'll be on the lookout for how Spotify's ad performance stacks up against peers in Q4.

All in all, given the very favorable calendar of price increases in 2026 versus 2025, Spotify looks well-positioned to rebound. We note that consensus is only calling for 14% revenue growth in FY26, which looks like an easy bar to cross when current Premium subs are growing at a 12% y/y pace, and when an 8% U.S. price increase will be in effect for ~11 months of FY26 without lapping any price increases in FY25. Stay long here and buy the dip.

data.title: AI Productivity, Employment And UBI
data.description:
It is expected that AI productivity increases will vastly transform the U.S. economy. Firms are utilizing AI productivity enhancements to automate repetitive tasks, and research and coding functions have already been implemented. The obvious problem is that when machines perform functions once done by humans, what are the humans supposed to do for income? This increase in AI productivity is measurable across various sectors, as supply chains operate more efficiently, data analysis accelerates, and customer service utilizes automated agents to streamline tasks. Manufacturing, once considered a stable sector of the economy, is increasingly using robotics to reduce labor costs. Professional services are also increasingly displacing workers in medical, legal, and other areas of the service economy to improve output (read: profits) per worker.

This is not a new thing. It has been accelerating since the invention of the fax machine and phone answering devices. The use of AI productivity-enhancing technology is becoming increasingly apparent. But as shown, the shift by corporations to focus on worker productivity is ongoing.

Recent corporate statements confirm this shift. At a 2025 financial conference, JPMorgan Chase reported that AI adoption doubled productivity gains in certain operations from 3% to 6%, with some roles seeing efficiency increases of 40% to 50%. Other banks said AI allows them to accomplish more work with the same headcount.

In theory, the promise of AI productivity increases is alluring. While firms can produce more with fewer inputs, humans will have more time to pursue education, leisure, and spend time with their families, increasing overall health and happiness. Again, that is theory, and the subject of today’s commentary.

Productivity Set To Surge

The strict definition of “productivity” is the output per unit of input. In other words, if output rises, it should correspond to an increase in employee compensation, as economic demand leads to the production of more products. Since 1947, a correlation has existed between economic output and the 3-month average of the annual rate of change in employee compensation.

Between 2004 and the pandemic, annual labor productivity growth averaged just 1.5% per year, significantly below the pace required for sustained real wage improvement. Recent gains measured in 2023 showed a temporary uptick; however, whether this marks a trend driven by AI rather than short-term business cycles remains unclear.

Furthermore, emerging research suggests that AI has the potential to deliver significant productivity improvements. A study of generative AI usage found that average workers using tools like ChatGPT completed tasks 40% faster with higher quality, implying substantial productivity enhancements when AI is integrated into work processes. The Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis estimated that generative AI contributed a roughly 1.1% boost to aggregate productivity, with individual workers saving multiple hours per week on routine tasks. Lastly, a TIME-published analysis of Anthropic research suggests that AI has the potential to double U.S. labor productivity growth, increasing it by approximately 1.8% if widespread adoption occurs.

These projections also align with broader institutional forecasts. The IMF reports that AI could significantly impact nearly 40% of jobs worldwide, presenting both opportunities and risks for income growth and inequality. Yet, productivity gains alone do not automatically lead to wage increases or employment growth.

The Problem

The problem arises when productivity increases without a corresponding demand for labor. AI operates without downtime, 24/7, and does not require traditional wages, benefits, or breaks. If AI performs tasks that previously employed millions of workers, the question of how displaced workers earn income becomes central. Corporate leaders acknowledge this challenge. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has highlighted the unpredictability of AI’s impact, noting that productivity gains may come with labor market disruptions that current policy tools are ill-equipped to manage.

Historical examples show how technological shifts displace workers in the short term. For instance, during the “Industrial Revolution,” artisans lost jobs to mechanized production. Horse-drawn carriage drivers disappeared with the advent of automobiles. Yes, workers eventually moved into new fields, but the transition involved hardship and community upheaval. Automation in prior eras often created new kinds of jobs, but the pace and breadth of AI disruption could set this wave apart. Instead of merely replacing manual labor, AI now substitutes for tasks across both blue-collar and white-collar jobs. Research by Oxford economists Carl Frey and Michael Osborne highlighted that many occupations have tasks that are susceptible to automation, and could disappear entirely.

Compounding the challenge, since the late 1970s, productivity gains started diverging from typical worker compensation. According to the Economic Policy Institute, productivity growth far outpaced wage growth for the median worker, signaling that gains from technology and economic expansion have accrued disproportionately to capital owners and high-skill labor. This productivity-pay gap signals that, even before AI’s full impact arrives, workers were not sharing equitably in productivity-driven prosperity.

The pace of technological change means millions of Americans face an uncertain labor market. Young workers entering the workforce find fewer traditional hiring pathways and rising expectations around digital and AI-related skills. Older workers frequently lack the time or resources to retrain in rapidly shifting skill environments. Across age groups, employers deploying AI experience reduced labor costs and increased productivity, which simultaneously puts pressure on wages and job security.

The reality is stark. The economy may grow, but how the gains are distributed will determine whether everyday Americans thrive or struggle. Without structural policy interventions, technological displacement risks widening income inequality and weakening labor market attachment. The promise of more leisure, education, and family time from productivity gains remains theoretical. If workers lack stable incomes, employment opportunities, or bridging support, the rest won’t matter.

But, this is where the “cries for UBI” become most vocal.

The Wrong Solution

Legendary investor Howard Marks has described AI’s impact on employment as “terrifying.” He emphasized that work provides purpose and identity beyond mere income. Notably, he stated that “…financial support alone will not replace the psychological and social benefits of employment.” That is a crucially important statement, which we now have the data to support. Universal Basic Income (UBI) is the default proposal to offset the impacts of increased AI productivity. The logic sounds simple enough: “If AI displaces workers, send checks to households to replace lost wages and economic stability returns.”

The problem is that the evidence does not support this conclusion.

Following the pandemic-driven shutdown of the economy, we sent checks to households, which was a form of Universal Basic Income. Many articles espoused the benefits of such an operation, but the results were far less appealing. Surging inflation eroded the benefits of the stimulus and left Americans far worse off than they would have been otherwise. However, other real-time tests have also yielded less than promising outcomes.

We previously discussed one of the UBI experiments, which found predictable results. Short-term relief did not translate into higher employment, improved skills, or long-term income growth. Cash transfers temporarily increased consumption but did not raise productivity, increase labor force participation, or improve economic mobility.

“Participants in the study generally did not use the extra time to seek new or better jobs—even though younger participants were slightly more likely to pursue additional education. There was no clear indication that the participants in the study were more likely to take the risk of starting a new business, although Vivalt points out that there was a significant uptick in “precursors” to entrepreneurialism. Instead, the largest increases were in categories that the researchers termed social and solo leisure activities.”

The Argument magazine also reviewed multiple studies on guaranteed income and reached a similar conclusion. While recipients reported lower stress and higher short-term satisfaction, these gains faded quickly. Employment outcomes showed little improvement, job search intensity declined in several cases, and participation in education and retraining did not rise significantly.

In other words, giving people money without purpose helped much less than promised.

The core flaw in UBI is structural, as it treats income as the problem. Employment is the real issue. Yes, work provides wages, but it also offers skill development, social structure, and a sense of purpose, along with long-term stability. A simple check replaces none of those, and unfortunately, as 2020 shows, when producers realize that checks are being sent, they raise prices to capitalize on it. In other words, an artificial increase in incomes will quickly be absorbed by higher prices (inflation), effectively rendering the UBI useless.

Here is the most critical point.

“An economy cannot function on transfers alone; production must precede consumption. UBI reverses this order.“

Cost also matters. A national UBI program large enough to offset AI-driven displacement would require trillions of dollars annually. Funding such a program would either require higher taxes, debt expansion, or both. While each option will reduce future growth, higher taxes reduce investment incentives, while increased debt raises interest costs and crowds out private capital. Neither path supports long-term prosperity.

UBI also weakens the labor signal. Wages communicate where labor is needed, and training follows opportunity. UBI dulls this signal by separating income from work, and, over time, workforce attachment erodes, skills decay, and reentry into employment becomes increasingly complex. This dynamic showed up repeatedly in pilot programs.

Most importantly, UBI avoids the hard work of reform. It sidesteps education reform, workforce retraining, mobility assistance, and pro-growth labor policy. It accepts displacement as inevitable and permanent. History shows this approach fails, and past technological shifts succeeded because workers moved into new roles. In other words, policy supported adaptation, not withdrawal.

AI productivity gains will demand active solutions, not government gifts. Skill development, apprenticeships, employer-based training, wage insurance, and mobility support. These tools address displacement directly, while UBI does not.

Defaulting to UBI is an admission of policy failure and signals surrender to the disruption rather than managing it. The United States grew prosperous by expanding opportunity, not replacing work with checks. That lesson remains relevant today as AI continues to reshape the economy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why Joby Aviation Is A Prime Pick For Speculative Investors
data.description:
An investment in Joby Aviation (JOBY) represents a once-in-a-generation opportunity to invest in a pioneer of the nascent eVTOL (electric Vertical Take-Off and Landing) air taxi industry, one of the fastest-growing industries in the U.S.

Grand View Research forecasts the eVTOL industry to grow at a 54.9% compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") between 2024 and 2030. Since Joby is one of two companies that industry observers consider furthest ahead in capitalizing on the industry's forecasted explosive growth, investors expect the stock to produce long-term market-beating returns.

Since I wrote my October 29, 2025, article on Joby, the stock price has made little headway; it is down 7.18% from the price at the time of publication, as of the January 9 closing price.

Despite news that the company is making substantial progress toward launching a commercial air taxi service and a potential catalyst for near-term upside, investors have reservations concerning the company issuing equity to fund its dash to the finish line for full Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Type Certification and getting its electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) into the air with paying passengers.

On October 9, 2025, Joby announced it was raising an estimated $591.0 million in equity (the actual equity raise totaled $576 million). The stock dropped 5% on October 10, closing at $16.28, as investors expressed displeasure over being diluted by an equity raise. Joby stock has struggled to gain traction since that date.

The dilutive equity raise, along with disappointing third-quarter 2025 earnings results released on November 5, has had a significant negative impact on the stock. Joby's third-quarter 2025 earnings per share ("EPS") loss of 0.48 missed analysts' estimates by a wider than expected $0.29. The stock dropped by 4.5% to $14.32 at the November 6 close.

Investors are concerned that even if Joby gets its air taxis off the ground and generates revenue, it's spending so much money today that it could take longer for the company to become profitable. The market hates unprofitability because it brings uncertainty to a company's long-term future. Eventually, if a company is unable to find a path to profitability, it will either fail or need to raise additional capital, and the market doesn't like either scenario.

However, note that the $229.149 million loss from changes in the fair value of warrants and earnout shares accounted for a significant portion of the company's $401.226 million net loss. The loss on the warrants' fair value resulted from an increase in the stock price, triggering a revaluation and a non-cash charge. If you understand GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) mark-to-market accounting of warrants, you understand that it is counterintuitive. When the company records a gain from warrants, it means the stock price is declining. A loss on warrants means the stock price is rising, signaling investors' confidence in the stock.

Although some investors are concerned about the company's net loss, growth investors often focus on adjusted EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) for early-stage, speculative companies, which strips out non-cash expenses, such as warrant-related expenses and stock-based compensation. Joby's third-quarter 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $132.765 million, which is the actual cash the company is spending to get its air taxis off the ground.

Joby is also now flush with cash after its October equity offering. So, the risk that the company may run out of cash may be lower than it appears from its EPS losses.

Although the stock was lackluster following the company's third-quarter earnings release, there was some good news signaling that it may be a worthwhile long-term investment. Joby made significant progress toward its ultimate goal of getting its eVTOL fleet off the ground and generating revenue. It remains the leader in achieving FAA type certification.

On November 5, Joby began power-on testing of several aircraft, marking the start of the final step required for the company to receive a Type Inspection Authorization ("TIA"), which no other U.S.-based eVTOL manufacturer has achieved.

The following shows the company's progress in its certification stages as of October 31, 2025.

Before we go any further, it is important to understand the difference between flight testing and a TIA, because the company often refers to both on earnings calls, and some might confuse the two terms, believing they refer to the same thing, which is not the case.

When a company uses a pilot to test-fly an aircraft during a test that does not count toward FAA certification, it is a simple flight test. Joby often makes press releases when it completes a test flight. For instance, it held its first test flight between two U.S. public airports on November 15, 2025, between Marina (OAR) and Monterey (MRY). It held a second test flight between two U.S. airports on October 6, 2025, from Marina to Salinas at the California International Airshow. Joby also runs international test flights. It completed the first piloted air taxi flight between two locations in the UAE on November 17, 2025, from Margham to Al Maktoum International Airport (DWC), ahead of the Dubai Airshow.

In contrast, a TIA is not an actual flight test but an FAA authorization that allows FAA inspectors and FAA test pilots to conduct official certification flight testing of a new aircraft design. A TIA is technically the start of the stage 4 testing and analysis stage. After a company receives its TIA, inspectors can officially inspect the aircraft and verify that it complies with certification plans established in stage 3. After inspectors verify that the aircraft complies with the aircraft type's certification plans, FAA test pilots can conduct flight testing that counts toward certification.

Stage 5 is the phase in which the FAA reviews and authenticates the stage 4 results and performs final compliance checks. Once the FAA completes its stage 5 assessment, it will issue Joby a Type Certificate, which means the manufacturer can legally manufacture the plane, and the FAA considers the plane type airworthy. Once the FAA issues a Type Certificate, the company will be close to beginning commercial operations and generating revenue from key operations.

However, although type certification is a major obstacle for Joby to overcome, it doesn't mean the company can immediately scale up commercial air operations. As I explained in my January 7, 2025, article on Joby, it will also need Part 135 certification, a section of the Federal Aviation Regulations that outlines the requirements for operating smaller aircraft for commercial purposes. The company must also follow Part 5 Air Operations Safety Management Systems ("SMS") of the Federal Aviation Regulations, which requires an organization to implement an aviation SMS. The company must also train and qualify pilots and establish FAA-certified maintenance facilities.

A Few News Items to Get Excited About

The eVTOL industry is fortunate that the U.S. government currently favors its development. Although I just stated that Type Certification doesn't mean Joby can immediately scale up commercial air operations, it doesn't mean the company can't start operating air taxi services in a few selected locations as a test run.

Chief Executive Officer JoeBen Bevirt said on the third quarter 2025 earnings call:

U.S. government's recently announced eIPP [Electric Vertical Takeoff and Landing and Advanced Air Mobility Integration Pilot Program]. Through an executive order, the President has directed the Department of Transportation and the FAA to ensure that mature eVTOL aircraft can begin operations in select markets in the U.S. ahead of full FAA certification.

The company is already negotiating with various state and local governments to participate in this precertification program. What eIPP means for Joby is that once its eVTOL receives a TIA, it may become eligible to begin limited commercial operations, allowing it to generate revenue sooner. It is like a running head start for its commercial air taxi business, allowing the company to build operational experience, infrastructure, and an eVTOL fleet before the full FAA certification process is complete. Joby is already ramping up eVTOL production. CEO Bevirt said on the third-quarter earnings call:

We see the eIPP program pulling early demand for our aircraft forward. Taken alongside the demand we're also seeing from a range of international early adopters, it's clear we'll need to keep accelerating production if we are to keep up with the incredible demand we're seeing for our product.

A side benefit of eiPP is that it guarantees early demand from cities and states for its air services, giving Joby a reason to rapidly ramp up eVTOL production before full Type and Part 135 Certification. Rapid scaling of eVTOL production is preferable to a slow ramp, as it can help lower eVTOL unit costs more quickly, accelerating time to profitability.

Additionally, eIPP allows Joby to see where the heaviest demand for its services is coming from and to choose the premium locations for its initial air taxi service. Since there is limited space for air taxi infrastructure in any given location, the first air taxi company in that location will generally have a monopoly or, at worst, a duopoly by being the first to establish infrastructure and air routes. So, eIPP can help Joby lock in the most profitable routes for its services.

Last, but not least, eIPP will allow investors to get an early look at the financials of air taxi operations. Up until now, forward-looking estimates of revenue and profitability have been theoretical and speculative. If and when eIPP flights get off the ground, investors will be better able to assess public acceptance of air taxis, the prices air taxi operators can charge, and the profitability of each flight. Analysts should be able to build better financial models, and revenue and profitability estimates should become more reliable. Investors can then value the core business based on potential revenue and earnings growth, rather than price-to-book (P/B).

If the financials paint a pretty picture of the future and some of the uncertainty about what to expect is removed from the business, investors may be more willing to bid the stock price up. If Joby is chosen for eIPP, the stock is likely to rise. As a result, Joby's potential receipt of a TIA and eIPP approval is a near-term catalyst for the stock price.

There was also great news for long-term investors. The CEO revealed that Joby eventually plans to make air taxi flights autonomous (without a pilot). Specifically, he said on the third quarter 2025 earnings call:

While our initial air taxi product will launch with a fully qualified commercial pilot on board, I believe, a future where we are able to take the pilot out of the loop is approaching more rapidly than I expected even 6 months ago.

For decades, the principal barrier to commercial autonomy hasn't been technical or regulatory. It's been the way our commercial air traffic control system works. The current system still requires a human being on a radio to speak with another human being to deconflict airspace, file flight plans and confirm departures and arrivals. The current administration has indicated its intent to invest in modernizing this approach...

Although some may interpret the CEO Bevirt's comment above as meaning that the FAA is going fully digital and completely eliminating human-to-human radio traffic, that is not true.

The FAA's new NextGen air traffic control system will not eliminate all voice radio. However, it will lessen dependence on voice radio by relying more on machine-to-machine communications rather than human-to-human radio for routine tasks such as flight‑plan filing, keeping planes at a safe distance, and integrating communication between autonomous and piloted aircraft in shared airspace. NextGen will enable low‑altitude aircraft, such as eVTOLs, to fly autonomously.

Joby is actively preparing for the day when autonomous eVTOL flight is allowed. In June 2024, Joby purchased Xwing's autonomy division. It is now using Xwing's autonomy division to autonomously test-fly a Cessna around Hawaii, monitoring the plane from Guam, over 3,000 miles away. So far, the test flight has logged over 7,000 miles.

Joby plans to integrate the same Xwing autopilot technology, Superpilot, that flew around Hawaii, with NVIDIA's (NVDA) IGX Thor platform. CEO Bevirt stated on the third-quarter earnings call:

Joby will be the aviation launch partner for NVIDIA's IGX Thor platform, which uses their Blackwell Architecture to help ingest extraordinary amounts of data in real time to support an even more performance and safe autonomy stack for our aircraft. Integrating this level of compute will help us maximize the potential of autonomous flight. We'll be able to deliver autonomous mission management that enables the aircraft to determine, request and follow optimal flight plans while adapting to changes in weather, air traffic control instructions or unexpected events.

Joby also plans to install Superpilot on the aircraft it is co-developing with L3Harris (LHX) for military and government applications.

Investors benefit if Joby can eliminate the pilot from the equation through automation, thereby transitioning Joby from a low-margin transportation service (like a traditional airline) into a high-margin technology platform. The company didn't indicate a timeline for autonomous air taxi service after becoming fully verified, likely because it depends on upgrades to the commercial air traffic control system.

Valuation

Now that the company has finally generated some revenue, its trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 533.97, well above the industrial sector median of 1.92 and the aerospace and industrial sector's P/S of 2.858. The company's relatively high P/S ratio reflects explosive revenue growth. However, this revenue growth comes off a very low base. As its revenue base grows, its revenue growth rate will slow, and eventually its P/S ratio will decline.

The eVTOL air taxi industry is in its very early stages. No companies are generating meaningful revenue by transporting customers. Joby's closest comparison, Archer Aviation (ACHR), has no revenue and no P/S ratio. So Joby's P/S ratio can't be compared with a similar company.

Although Joby generates revenue today, it doesn't come from its main business activities. The company's purchase of Blade Air Mobility's passenger business on August 29, 2025, a business already generating revenue before the acquisition, is currently Joby's main revenue source. Joby's revenue line will mostly become relevant when the source of that revenue comes primarily from its eVTOL air taxi business.

The following chart shows that Archer Aviation has a substantially higher tangible book value ("TBV") and book value per share. The market considers TBV as a proxy for a company's liquidation value.

I stated the following in my October article:

Theoretically, if management needed to liquidate these companies, Archer Aviation would provide a larger financial buffer against potential losses for creditors and investors. Additionally, the higher TBV is likely composed of cash, suggesting Archer has a longer cash runway to fund its operations, research and development, and certification process without raising capital immediately, thereby reducing the risk of dilution.

However, be careful: a higher TBV does not necessarily make a stock "safer." The market is balancing each eVTOL company's path to commercialization, management execution, technological advantage, and regulatory progress in assessing safety. So, while the TBV is important, you should also consider other factors when assessing the relative risks of investing in each company.

The following chart shows that Archer Aviation has $1.649 billion in cash and short-term investments, substantially more than Joby's $978.47 million at the end of September. However, the company's October equity raise of $576 million wasn't included in the third-quarter results, which ended on September 30, 2025. So, Joby actually has approximately $1.554 billion in cash.

Joby burned $532.75 million in trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash in the third quarter, compared with $486.70 million in TTM cash for Archer Aviation. Therefore, Joby has an approximately 2.91-year cash runway, up from 1.97 years in my previous article. In contrast, Archer Aviation has a 3.38-year cash runway. Because of Archer Aviation's longer cash runway, some may consider it a "safer" stock to invest in and less likely to engage in dilutive capital raises.

Still, the flaw in my cash burn assessment is that it assumes that cash burn stays static. If cash burn continues to increase, Joby's cash runway could be much shorter, and the company could return to raising additional dilutive equity more quickly than a 2.91-year cash runway implies. That is why some believe Joby doesn't deserve a premium valuation.

Additionally, investors evaluate each company's business model. Joby is a vertically integrated company entering its most capital-intensive phase—simultaneously scaling mass manufacturing, pursuing final FAA certification, and preparing for international commercial launches. Investors are very concerned about the cash Joby will need to spend to fund all those goals.

The market fears that the likely increase in cash burn will lead to a scenario in which the company will constantly make dilutive capital raises to finance operations. Goldman Sachs issued a Sell rating on Joby over its cash burn and valuation. A Seeking Alpha article quoted Goldman's opinion:

JOBY (Sell rating): "JOBY is the oldest company in the space with the most flight hours and a lead in certification. It is focused on being a one-stop shop (OEM, supplier, operator), which means it has the largest market opportunity, but we believe the operator model will have regulatory hurdles and significant capital requirements... The stock trades at a significant premium, which we don't believe is justified."

Archer Aviation doesn't face the same cash burn issues as Joby because it uses a capital-light business model, outsourcing its eVTOL manufacturing to Stellantis, thereby avoiding the hundreds of millions of dollars in costs to build and staff a factory. So, although Joby may be ahead of Archer in several areas, some investors prefer Archer because it is less likely to face financial issues and will likely pursue fewer dilutive capital raises.

The following is a 2027 P/S/G (price-to-sales-to-growth) ratio comparison between Joby and Archer Aviation. Investors typically consider a company with a higher P/S/G ratio as overvalued relative to a company with a lower P/S/G ratio. Additionally, analysts expect Archer to achieve annual profitability two years earlier than Joby.

Company name 2027 forward P/S 2027 forward revenue estimated growth rate One-year forward P/S to revenue growth rate Analysts' expected profitability for the year
Joby 67.77 84.02% 0.80 2031
Archer Aviation 21.16 854.51% 0.02 2029

So, there are reasons some consider Joby overvalued compared to Archer. However, until both companies begin flight operations and produce actual results, take these outlooks with a grain of salt.

Risks

Joby has significant execution risks, especially on the manufacturing side. One of the big issues is that Joby's Marina, California, site is only a pilot manufacturing facility with a capacity of 24 aircraft per year. The company designed the Marina site for initial production runs and to manufacture eVTOLs for FAA certification testing. Its mass-manufacturing eVTOL site is in Dayton, Ohio, with a capacity of up to 500 eVTOLs per year. The company plans to perfect the manufacturing process at the pilot site in California before replicating it in Ohio. However, if Joby encounters any delays perfecting the manufacturing process at its pilot site, it could delay its mass manufacturing efforts in Ohio. If mass manufacturing is delayed for any reason, the company may be unable to take advantage of its certification, international, and eIPP plans simultaneously because it won't have enough planes. The market would likely react harshly to any execution issues. Joby has de-risked this scenario by working closely with Toyota to utilize its manufacturing expertise. CEO Bevirt said on the earnings call:

We perfect the process here at our pilot facility in California, and then we're able to scale it alongside our partners with Toyota -- our partnership with Toyota, I would love to just highlight how vitally important Toyota is and how strong relationship that we have with Toyota right now. We have never been closer to Toyota. Toyota has never been more leaned in.

Joby's Blade acquisition also has execution risk. In my last Joby article, I stated:

Management expects the acquisition to provide Joby with a first-mover competitive advantage by immediately gaining Blade's 10 years of operational experience building an urban air mobility business, established routes, lounges, and a team in key U.S. and international markets, as well as access to over 50,000 passengers. Other companies trying to compete in the same markets will face immediate challenges in building the necessary infrastructure and customer base to compete against Joby.

Joby purchased Blade Air Mobility's passenger business on August 29, 2025. The remaining medical business is now known as Strata. Before Joby acquired Blade, the company's passenger business was historically unprofitable and had only recently turned profitable as of its first quarter 2025 results, generating only $54,000 in adjusted EBITDA, barely breakeven. The profitable part of the company was the medical business, which Strata retained.

In the last quarter (second quarter 2025) before the sale, Blade reported adjusted EBITDA of $2.39 million, up 205% year over year. The divested medical business, Strata, was far more profitable, with adjusted EBITDA of $6.04. So, what Joby acquired was a viable, low-margin business, not a high-margin profit engine.

Joby's strategy is to convert Blade’s modestly profitable 50,000-passenger helicopter-riding customer base into a scalable, high-margin business through its eVTOL air taxi business. The idea is that by replacing high-maintenance helicopters, which have high fuel costs, with electrically powered eVTOLs, Joby can reduce fuel and maintenance costs and increase margins. However, this plan relies heavily on transitioning customers from helicopters, a technology they are familiar with, to eVTOLs, a new, unfamiliar, and untested technology. There is no guarantee that Blade's customers will be willing to make the shift.

Another potential issue is that the pre-acquisition Blade and the current Joby have two distinct business models. Blade didn't own or lease the helicopters that the passengers flew in; it was asset-light. Blade operated under a business model somewhat similar to Uber's (UBER), meaning it used third-party operators who owned, maintained, and flew the helicopters passengers rode in. In contrast, Joby has an asset-heavy business model, in which it owns, builds, operates, and maintains its own eVTOL fleet. Theoretically, Joby's full-stack business model can produce higher margins by avoiding the need to pay a third-party operator. The disadvantage and risk of Joby's business model is its high fixed costs. Suppose demand for eVTOLs is lower than the company predicts, and fewer passengers use the service; Joby must still pay the high fixed costs, even though revenue is lower. Its profitability would suffer, and investors would likely sell off the stock.

Low profitability would be especially devastating during the eIPP ramp, as investors evaluate the business's financials for upcoming forecasts. If profitability is low, the market will likely assign the stock a lower valuation.

Although autonomy has significant long-term upside potential, investors should avoid buying Joby solely for its autonomous flight capabilities, as the company must overcome substantial regulatory obstacles in the commercial arena. Joby is focused on developing autonomous flight capabilities for the military first because it's easier to get autonomy approved for defense applications. Executive Chairman of the Board Paul Sciarra said on the third quarter earnings call:

The first instantiation on a Joby developed aircraft of autonomy is very likely to be the hybrid aircraft that I mentioned earlier on in the call. This is one that's obviously focused on defense applications. We think that's a perfect test bed for the stock, one, because it's already been well tested with its customer on conventional aircraft; and two, because there's not the same sort of regulatory rigor around certification for these sorts of platforms.

The last risk this article will cover is that investors' short-term expectations for autonomous flight may be too high.

The public may not initially fully accept autonomous aircraft, which may pressure regulators to implement a rigorous certification process for automated flight. As the public's perception regarding autonomous cars has been formed by crashes, an autonomous eVTOL crash could disillusion the public and set back approval of the automated flight concept for years.

Commercial autonomous flight could take longer than the company expects, and investors shouldn't price in the prospective gains from eliminating the pilot role. People who buy the stock solely because of the autonomous flight hype may be hurt if it takes longer than expected to become a reality.

Joby Remains a Buy for Aggressive Growth Investors Only

The company's stagnant stock price since my last article and its monthly standard deviation of nearly 99.35%, 6.4 times higher than the S&P 500, are signs of high stock volatility.

Conservative investors looking for reliable gains or dividends should avoid this early-stage, speculative stock. However, the company's progress toward commercializing an air taxi service indicates it is on the verge of moving from virtually zero revenue to potentially billions in revenue. The company is one of two companies, alongside Archer Aviation, that are early movers in the eVTOL taxi service industry. In the eVTOL industry, the early bird will likely catch the worm.

Once all premium locations and air routes are developed, it will be challenging for new competitors to later establish operations in the same locations. If air taxis generate strong public demand, the chances that they will be the sole air taxi option or will face only one other player are excellent, creating a situation in which they should be able to maintain their margins in a heavy-demand environment.

For investors willing to speculate that public demand for air taxis will materialize, Joby is one of the best choices due to its technical leadership, its leading position in the FAA certification process, its first-mover status in developing maintenance infrastructure, and its infrastructure for pilot training. These advantages justify an investment for aggressive growth investors with a high-risk tolerance. I maintain my buy recommendation for speculative investors.

data.title: National Health Investors: A Healthcare REIT Ready For The Senior Housing Supercycle
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

National Health Investors (NHI) is up quite a bit over the past year, as the company is actively improving, recovering and transitioning to a SHOP-oriented model, although I believe it still offers an opportunity to get a solid dividend and exposure to a very significant long-term trend in the senior housing industry.

As we can see, NHI reported an even better Normalized FAD of ~$62.25 million in Q3'25 (vs. $49.38 million in Q3'24), meaning about $1.31 per share compared to only $1.13 in Q3'24. That's a very significant increase despite the higher number of shares, as a result of a mix of factors, including collecting $5.1 million in repayments on previously deferred rent, SHOP transition (SHOP NOI up 63% YoY) and slightly lower interest expenses.

Still, for the full year, NHI improved their guidance quite a bit, expecting Normalized FAD of $231.7 million to $233.5 million compared to the previous guidance of $227.9 million to $229.8 million. At midpoint, that's roughly $232.6 million, placing them at a P/FAD ratio of ~16 based on the current ~$3.73 billion market cap, which is not bad given the overall business quality and growth potential in this market.

Financially, based on NHI's latest report, we see a good position for a REIT, with a relatively low amount of leverage, a decent cash position and a lot of assets to cover everything. Once again, the PB may seem low when the equity is at ~$1.49 billion compared to the ~$3.73 billion market cap, but we should keep in mind that these assets are not only depreciated for tax reasons, but their value is displayed at the price of acquisition, not adjusted for their real market value - which can be much higher even in today's market.

What's even more attractive is that NHI has a very impressive debt maturity schedule, with barely any debt due over the next half a decade, as most of it is due after 2029 - plus significant liquidity still available under their line of credit that should allow them to enjoy potential opportunities.

Dividends

After a few years of struggle due to the pandemic's obvious effects on this type of company, NHI is back to slowly growing its dividend, currently paying a yield of around 4.7%, representing roughly $175 million per year, for a payout of roughly 75.37% based on the midpoint FAD they estimate, which is very solid and definitely has plenty of room for upside if they want to even if they don't achieve that much growth.

Risks & Opportunities

REITs (especially healthcare ones) have been affected significantly in the years since the pandemic, as not only did the occupancy rates take a significant hit while costs rose and tenants had issues, but the cost of external capital also increased significantly as a result of the rate hikes.

This is ultimately normal, as rate hikes lead to higher treasury rates and SOFR, consequently causing higher costs of debt, while the market now has a much more attractive alternative if it wants a 4% to 5% return (the theoretically risk free treasuries), which also pushes the yield of REITs up as stock prices fall to adjust to the new risk-reward profile.

As a result, REITs, which have to pay most of what they make back to us through dividends, are suddenly pushed into an environment with higher debt costs, higher costs of issuing stock (the dividend is basically the yield they have to pay, similar to debt) as well as internal issues caused by tenants being late to pay rent or even filing for bankruptcies.

This is completely normal for the entire asset class, and I wanted to reiterate this because more and more people are calling REITs bad investments because of their performance in the past 2-5 years, which is a shame because I also believe these have been some of the best years to accumulate them.

As a side note, although I don't own NHI (for now), I do own Omega (OHI) with an average cost of $28.7 per share, meaning I am not only up about 54% since 2022 when I started buying, but I also locked in a sustainable yield of cost of about 9.33% - and that's without taking into account any potential increases. That's more than I could've asked from an asset like this, especially since it also offers excellent exposure to long-term trends, which I'll mention below.

However, although rate cuts can help, we should keep in mind that they aren't the only thing that dictates long-term rates, as the market factors in much more than just that, including the prospects of the country, its growth expectations, inflation, etc., which is where the issue might be.

Right now, there are several other factors that the market is worrying about even beyond the country's growth expectations, with the December Fed minutes showing a 9-3 vote in favor of the cut, which is the biggest disagreement since 2019, while some participants indicated that they believe a pause is the right call following this cut.

Meanwhile, we have rising fear around the Fed's independence, as Powell's term ends in May 2026 and Trump will likely nominate a person that shares his views this time. Although that's still just 1 vote out of 12 in FOMC decisions, we're still talking about a very influential person. Even if the market would not listen to what this person says, we would still lose important insights into the near-term that can lead to more volatility and rising uncertainty.

Especially following the DOJ criminal investigation into Powell, rising uncertainty around Greenland, Iran, Venezuela, tariffs, and other macro worries, we can see the market demanding better yields for the new risks it perceives, which would be directly reflected in the cost of capital of these companies, including NHI.

However, over the long-term - which should be the main focus when investing in a company like this - the trend is quite clearly in NHI's favor, in my opinion. As OHI also mentioned, we're talking about a very fragmented market, meaning that they should have plenty of room for acquisitions, with their status as a public company allowing them to issue stock and tap into external capital that's not available for a private company, allowing them to enjoy the massive potential boom in demand from Baby Boomers. As NHI's CEO also mentioned during their Q3 Earnings Call:

The momentum at NHI is building. We are well positioned and laser-focused to capitalize on the generational growth in the senior housing industry over the next decade. As I noted last quarter, we have methodically invested in creating a strong foundation across all of our disciplines that will allow us to significantly expand our presence in private pay senior housing, where we see the greatest risk-adjusted returns.
