Meanwhile, the transition to SHOP and basically cutting tenants as they focus on managing these properties themselves also limits the exposure to these companies (if they play it right, they can only keep the healthier tenants) while improving margins, although it also increases the operational risks.

The very important point is that there is plenty of room for occupancy to grow from the current ~87.5%, even though it improved over the past couple of years, and self-management can allow them to fully enjoy a potential growth in occupancy as demand rises, which wouldn't really make a meaningful difference if they were just simply renting these properties.

In other words, the rent is basically the same for their properties no matter if the occupancy is 75% or 100%, while managing these themselves basically allows them to get exposure to a potential increase or decrease.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diverse set of peers such as Omega Healthcare Investors, CareTrust REIT (CTRE), Welltower (WELL), Ventas (VTR), Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA) and LTC Properties (LTC), we see an overall average valuation, although most of these peers outperformed NHI in recent years, although it's worth noting that NHI has one of lowest levels of leverage and is in a good financial position.

Risks Reiterated

Despite the company's financial health and potential for more internal improvements, we should remember that the focus on SHOP can also increase their operational risks, exposing them even more to the environment they play in.

Meanwhile, even though the long-term should be in favor of this industry, the environment can still get even worse before it improves, while elevated external capital costs can limit the company's expansion potential, especially as they are directly impacted by macro factors even more as they are switching to SHOP.

Valuation

Starting with NHI's guidance of normalized FAD of $232.6 million in 2025, let's assume a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (similar to consensus FFO estimates) thanks to their switch to SHOP, potential occupancy/margin improvements and other expansion activities, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a 9% discount rate and 47.623 million shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $4.13 billion, for an intrinsic value of $86.69, which is still above the current ~$79.23.

Conclusion

I rate NHI a Buy thanks to their healthy financials, very manageable debt schedule and attractive dividend, as well as significant long-term potential thanks to macro tailwinds and SHOP focus. The overall risk-reward seems to be in the investment's favor, as even though they are exposed to industry-specific risks, the long-term tailwinds and solid dividend could make this quite rewarding, especially for a long-term dividend/pension-oriented portfolio.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Quality Dividend Growth Stocks Getting Way Too Cheap
data.description:
Right now, the market is offering up some very attractive opportunities to buy quality dividend growth stocks (SCHD) at deep discounts due to negative headlines, even though the underlying fundamentals remain sound. Today’s article will highlight two of these names in particular. While they are very different businesses from each other and are selling off due to different headlines, they share three things in common: strong cash flow visibility, conservatively structured balance sheets, and substantial valuation dislocations that make them very hard to ignore.

Invitation Homes and the Policy Fear Driving Its Sell-off

The first opportunity to talk about is Invitation Homes (INVH), which has sold off sharply due to headwinds from rising interest rates (which has slowed their ability to acquire properties at attractive spreads) and has fallen another leg lower recently on fears that the Trump administration’s push to ban large institutions from buying single-family homes could effectively break their growth model, which relies on acquiring new homes to grow the portfolio and ultimately cash flow per share.

INVH is the largest publicly traded single-family rental REIT (VNQ) in the United States, as it owns about 86,000 homes and manages 16,000 additional single-family properties. It primarily focuses its homeownership on infill, high-growth Sunbelt, and coastal markets. This boosts it due to strong in-migration in these regions.

Additionally, the business benefits from a structural housing undersupply and a market dynamic in which homeownership has never been more expensive due to the combination of high housing prices and high mortgage rates. Thus, renting is hundreds of dollars per month cheaper than owning in INVH’s main markets.

As a result, it is enjoying solid organic growth with a 2.1% blended rental rate growth, a 4.3% renewal rent growth rate, and a high occupancy rate of about 95.9%. Additionally, there is a build-to-rent delivery decline, which is further boosting supply-demand dynamics in INVH’s favor.

INVH also generates very stable cash flows thanks to its well-diversified portfolio of essential, affordable housing real estate, which has fairly inelastic demand, given that everyone needs housing, and renting a home is so much cheaper than buying a home right now.

Additionally, the company’s balance sheet is fairly conservatively positioned with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.2 times, which is below management’s long-term target range of 5.5 times to 6.0 times. This gives them additional flexibility to be able to invest opportunistically, either in stock buybacks or acquisitions, as well as be able to support the dividend even if the business were to encounter a cash flow dip.

Additionally, even if, in a worst-case scenario, they were banned from buying new homes, they could use their balance sheet flexibility to take advantage of their very cheap valuation, which currently stands at around a 30% discount to net asset value, to buy back stock aggressively and create a significant amount of NAV per share accretion for shareholders. Not only that, but they could pursue a partial or full liquidation of their portfolio as well, which would be very value accretive relative to their current stock price.

The fact that their retained cash flow right now is between 2.5% and 3% of market cap for a full year means they could buy back a decent chunk of stock if they did not have any other place to reinvest that capital. Meanwhile, it will also pay out a pretty solid 4.5% dividend yield, which is its highest yield since going public in 2017 and nearly twice its historical average dividend yield of about 2.5%. Additionally, it is priced at an FFO multiple of about 15.8 times, which is very cheap compared to its historical average of 23.8 times.

When you combine the quality of the underlying performance, the strong balance sheet, and the attractive long-term fundamentals in its markets, as well as its very cheap valuation and attractive dividend yield, INVH presents very attractive risk-adjusted return potential for shareholders. While the near-term story may be suppressed due to negative headlines, over the long term, the resilient intrinsic value of its holdings, the attractive dividend yield, the stable cash flow profile, and its deep discount to net asset value mean it should deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns.

Hess Midstream’s Fee-based Model and Natural Gas Growth Tilt

The next opportunity I am going to discuss is Hess Midstream (HESM), which is a midstream (AMLP) company that, while traditionally structured as an MLP, issues a 1099 tax form. HESM has pulled back sharply over concerns that its future earnings power could be impaired if CVX reduces operations in the Bakken, where HESM is concentrated.

This concern was heightened when HESM recently reduced its oil operations in the Bakken from previous expectations, leading to HESM pulling back slightly on its guidance.

In July of last year, HESM was guiding for "greater than $1.25B of financial flexibility expected through 2027 for potential incremental share repurchases." However, today, that guidance has been tempered to "~$1.0B of Adjusted Free Cash Flow after Distributions through 2028 for incremental shareholder returns and debt repayment." That means that, in addition to a reduction of $250M in the number, the focus has shifted from purely being on share repurchases to both shareholder returns and debt repayment. This signals a more cautious approach to capital allocation, which also seems to convey a growing uncertainty over the future production profile feeding HESM's infrastructure.

That being said, in our view, the sell-off is overblown. HESM is a fee-based Bakken-focused midstream operator that has 90% of its revenues protected by minimum volume commitments with long-term contracts that extend through 2033 in some cases. Additionally, its cash flows are 100% fee-based, so it has no direct commodity price exposure, and its minimum volume commitments are set at 80% of its three-year forward nominations.

Not only that, but gas represents 75% of its revenues, which gives it a growth tilt, since natural gas demand is growing, especially relative to crude. Not only that, but it also has a water gathering business that provides additional diversification alongside its crude and gas businesses. The company is also generating solid growth with expected 10% year-over-year EBITDA growth based on its full-year guidance, and its Q3 adjusted EBITDA improved sequentially from Q2.

Moreover, its CapEx is expected to fall substantially moving forward as it removed the CapEx gas plant from its forward plans, and Chevron (CVX) has announced less crude oil drilling activity in the Bakken, which means fewer operations on that front and an increasing tilt toward natural gas. This means that it should be able to generate substantial free cash flow in the near future, which it plans to use to continue growing its distribution at at least a 5% annual rate while also buying back stock aggressively. In Q3 alone, it bought back $100 million worth of stock and increased its distribution 2.4% sequentially.

Meanwhile, its balance sheet is in great shape with leverage at just ~3x. On a next 12-month basis, its dividend yield is very attractive at 9% and is very well covered by free cash flow. Not only that, but it trades at one of the lowest EV-to-EBITDA multiples in the midstream sector and also has among the very best free cash flow yields.

This presents a disconnect because the market is pricing it as if it is a rapidly declining business, and yet it is actually growing with a growth tilt toward natural gas. Yes, there is some risk given its concentration in the Bakken and the fact that it is heavily dependent on CVX, but all signs point to continued growth on the net when factoring in its natural gas assets, while its oil assets appear to be stable for the near future. Even if its oil assets do decline in the outer years, continued growth in natural gas should help to offset this.

Moreover, it has a huge margin of safety built into its valuation, with a strong balance sheet, aggressive buybacks, and dividend growth, thereby offsetting much of its concentration risk and potential weakened growth outlook beyond the next three to five years.

The biggest thing to monitor moving forward with HESM is what the minimum volume commitment framework will look like after 2028, including the potential for declined activity by CVX in the Bakken. However, its continued investments in growing its natural gas production in the region bode well for at least HESM’s natural gas business continuing to thrive in the years to come.

Investor Takeaway

While both of these businesses have fallen recently due to headline risk, namely CVX’s reduced production outlook for oil in the Bakken and INVH slumping in the wake of Trump’s potential appeal on house purchases by major institutions, both companies remain fundamentally sound and growing, with attractive, well-covered dividends, a path toward highly accretive buybacks, solid balance sheets, and ultimately large margins of safety to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns for patient investors. These are the sorts of opportunities I pounce on in my 7% to 8% yielding, low-beta, and outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Quantum Corporation: High-Risk Turnaround Play - Speculative Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Quantum Corporation, or "Quantum" (NASDAQ:QMCO), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Twelve months ago, I initiated coverage of beleaguered data storage solutions provider Quantum Corporation with a "Sell" rating after the stock was inadvertently picked up by speculative investors and traders as a perceived quantum computing play.

This turned out to be a great call, as the stock price has cratered by almost 90% over the past year:

However, it might be time to change my assessment of the company after Quantum announced better-than-expected preliminary Q3/FY2026 results on Thursday with sales coming in above the high end of the guidance range provided by management in the Q2 earnings release.

(...) Based on unaudited financials, the Company expects the following:

  • Revenue of approximately $72.7 million, above the high-end of the guided range of $67 million, plus or minus $2 million
  • GAAP gross margin of approximately 38%
  • GAAP operating expenses of approximately $28.1 million
  • Non-GAAP adjusted operating expenses of approximately $26.9 million, within the provided guidance range

Quantum expects to report its full results for the fiscal third quarter of 2026 by mid-February 2026.

Over the past year, the company has been busy dealing with accounting issues that required the restatement of past financial results.

Following several quarters of operational underperformance, Quantum also decided to replace senior management.

That said, sales execution started to improve in Q2/FY2026 with backlog increasing to approximately $25 million compared to the company's historical target range of $8 million to $10 million with strong momentum across all product lines as outlined by management on the conference call in November.

Based on Quantum's preliminary results, I would expect Adjusted EBITDA to be in a range of $3 million to $4 million, well above management's guidance of "positive $1 million, plus or minus $1 million".

Last month, Quantum shareholders approved a debt restructuring that resulted in the exchange of $54.7 million in term debt held by Dialectic Technology SPV LLC ("Dialectic") into new convertible notes despite less-than-stellar terms (emphasis added by author):

The Convertible Notes will mature on the third anniversary of the closing and will have an interest rate of 10% per annum, payable in kind, compounded annually.

The initial conversion price of the Convertible Notes will be $10.00 per share of common stock (Conversion Price), subject to adjustment for stock splits and similar events.

The Conversion Price is also subject to adjustment on the last day of each calendar quarter for three consecutive quarters following closing, but in any event the Conversion Price will not be reset below $4.00 per share of common stock.

Following the six-month anniversary of closing, if certain conditions are met, the Company may elect to require the conversion of a portion of the total outstanding amount of any Convertible Notes into shares of common stock at the then current Conversion Price.

Effective after the close of business on December 31, the conversion price was adjusted to $7.805 based on the 30-day value-weighted average trading price ("VWAP") of the company's common share preceding the reset date.

Following the price adjustment, Dialectic would receive approximately 7.0 million new common shares upon conversion.

Please note that Dialectic also extracted dilution-protected "Forbearance Warrants" to purchase 2.65 million new common shares at an initial exercise price of $8.81.

The exercise price and the number of shares underlying the Forbearance Warrant are subject to adjustment in the event of specified events, including dilutive issuances at a price lower than the exercise price of the Forbearance Warrant, a subdivision or combination of the common stock, a reclassification of the common stock or specified dividend payments, subject to certain limitations as set forth in the Forbearance Warrant. Upon exercise, the aggregate exercise price may be paid, at the Selling Stockholder's election, in cash or on a net issuance basis, based upon the then current market price of the common stock at the time of exercise.

In layman's terms, share issuances to Dialectic upon note conversions would lower the exercise price of the warrants.

Assuming full conversion and exercise of all warrants, Dialectic's stake in the company would amount to 41.3% based on 13.72 million outstanding common shares as of November 11, 2025.

Given the terms of the convertible notes and forbearance warrants, Dialectic would be incentivized to short Quantum's common stock in order to maximize gains. That said, short interest has actually declined in recent months:

For valuation purposes, I will stick with the current conversion price of $7.805, which, in combination with assumed warrant exercises, would result in outstanding shares increasing to 23.385 million. Please note that under this scenario, Quantum would receive an estimated $20.7 million in cash proceeds.

However, it is more likely that Dialectic would exercise the warrants on a net issuance basis, which means that Quantum would issue a lower amount of shares equal to the intrinsic value of the warrant but not receive any cash proceeds.

In addition, my valuation exercise does not assume additional share issuances under the company's Standby Equity Purchase Agreement ("SEPA") with a division of Yorkville Advisors Global.

Moreover, my estimates assume continued operational improvement in FY2027 and FY2028, with headwinds from skyrocketing memory prices being more than offset by increasing demand for the company's cold data storage solutions.

Should Quantum's turnaround take hold, I would expect market participants to value the company at an EV/Adjusted EBITDA multiple in a range of 10x to 15x, which is still significantly below much larger competitors from the likes of Pure Storage (PSTG), Seagate Technology (STX), and Western Digital Corporation (WDC).

At 12x estimated FY2028 EV/Adjusted EBITDA, the price target would calculate to $12.50. Keep in mind that my valuation is based on rather optimistic assumptions regarding potential dilution and the company's ability to execute on its turnaround efforts.

However, it is important to note that analysts currently expect Q4 revenues to decline by approximately 15% on a quarter-over-quarter basis, which I consider way too pessimistic despite some anticipated seasonality.

Should management's Q4 guidance exceed moderate consensus expectations significantly and backlog remain strong, more speculative investors and traders could be attracted to the stock, particularly with the company's name suggesting a focus on the still red-hot quantum computing space. However, while Quantum recently announced a quantum computing collaboration, its primary focus remains on data storage solutions.

Please note also that a rising share price would help keep convertible note dilution in check.

In sum, only the most speculative investors should consider an investment in this early stage of a potential turnabout and ahead of management's initial fourth quarter guidance next month.

Will certainly a high-risk investment, I am upgrading Quantum Corporation's shares from "Sell" to "Speculative Buy" with a price target of $12.50.

Risks

As outlined above already, an investment in the shares at this early stage of a potential turnaround should be considered highly speculative, particularly given uncertainties regarding the impact of skyrocketing memory prices on the company's business. Should management warn of margin pressures and lower demand for the company's flash storage products without an offsetting increase in sales for the company's cold data storage solutions, the stock price would likely drop to new 52-week lows.

Bottom Line

Following many years of sales declines and resulting cash outflows, Quantum Corporation's business has been showing signs of life in recent months.

On Thursday, the company announced better-than-expected preliminary Q3/FY2026 results, which should translate into meaningfully positive Adjusted EBITDA.

To continue its turnaround, Quantum will have to execute flawlessly in order to balance risks from skyrocketing memory prices impacting its more advanced flash storage products with a potential resurgence in demand for the company's cold data storage offerings.

In addition, the overhang from convertible notes and warrants recently issued to Dialectic is likely to persist in the near- to medium term.

Moreover, there might be additional dilution from sales under the above-discussed SEPA agreement.

On the flip side, consensus expectations for the final quarter of fiscal year 2026 appear to be on the low side, thus increasing the chance of management providing upside guidance next month.

With so many moving parts, the company remains a high-risk investment only suited for highly speculative investors and traders, particularly at this early stage of a potential turnaround. Put should new management indeed return the business to sustained growth and improved margins, upside in the shares could be north of 50% and potentially even higher.

As a result, I am upgrading Quantum Corporation's shares from "Sell" to "Speculative Buy" with a price target of $12.50.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Power Producers Spark Higher
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump to push for emergency auction to get tech firms funding new power plants. (00:27) Disney's (DIS) Kennedy hands off 'Star Wars' leadership. (02:03) Dimon says ‘no chance’ on Fed chair job. (03:00)

This is an abridged transcript.

Power producers such as Constellation Energy (CEG) and Vistra (VST) and NRG Energy (NRG) are on our biggest movers list this morning.

According to multiple media reports, The White House is expected to issue a directive today to propose an emergency power auction in which tech companies would bid for new plants built.

According to WSJ, citing people familiar with the matter, under the directive, power grid operator PJM Interconnection will be told to hold a power auction for tech companies to bid on 15-year contracts to build new power plants.

PJM is the largest grid operator in the U.S., spanning 13 states. Data centers built amid the AI boom have strained grid capacity and raised costs in recent power auctions.

The White House said its National Energy Dominance Council and the governors of several states, including Pennsylvania, Ohio, and Virginia, want to try to push the grid operator to take urgent steps to boost energy supply and curb price hikes.

CEG is up 1.1% after rising 3.2% on Thursday. Vistra is up 2% after seeing a 6.6% increase and NRG is up 1.8% after closing 5.8% higher.

It's time for a new wave to pick up the lightsaber for Disney (DIS), as Kathleen Kennedy is stepping down from her longtime position as the sensei of Star Wars.

Kennedy is departing Lucasfilm, where she's held a leadership position for nearly 14 years. She joined the production company as co-chair with Star Wars creator/director George Lucas, and became its only leader shortly afterward when Disney (DIS) bought the company and Lucas exited.

Dave Filoni, chief creative officer and a Lucas protégé, will take over creative leadership of Lucasfilm, while President/General Manager Lynwen Brennan will act as co-president and lead the business side. They'll report to Disney Entertainment Co-Chairman Alan Bergman.

Kennedy plans to return to full-time producing, including finishing upcoming Lucasfilm projects The Mandalorian and Grogu and Star Wars: Starfighter.

Jamie Dimon, chief executive officer of JPMorgan Chase (JPM), indicated he has no interest in becoming chair of the Federal Reserve.

Asked whether he would consider the role, Dimon replied there was “absolutely, positively no chance—no way, no how, for any reason.”

He was more open, however, to the idea of leading the U.S. Treasury. "The president calls you up and asks you to do something – you should consider it, so I would take the call and consider it," he said. "I like my job, and I’ve been my own boss for pretty much 25 years."

Dimon also said he wants to remain CEO of JPMorgan Chase (JPM) for at least another five years.

Dimon, who is 69 years old, has led the bank since 2006 and is one of the longest-serving CEOs on Wall Street.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

France warns U.S. against Greenland seizure as Europe sends troops to island

U.S. megabanks reduce headcount by most in nearly a decade - report

Tech earnings should be AI validation sign: Wedbush

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1% at $59/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.3% at $95,000. Gold is down 0.2% at $4,607.

The FTSE 100 is little changed and the DAX is up 0.2%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT) -4.9% Shares slipped after mixed results. The logistics company earned a better-than-expected profit despite a 2% drop in revenue.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Housing Market Index

Another item to add to your calendar but it’s not for today. It’s our Investing Experts Live-Top Ideas for 2026 event. Investing Experts is the sister podcast to Wall Street Breakfast. But this is a live event. It starts at noon on January 27th. You’ll hear from experts about where growth and income opportunities are emerging this year. They’ll discuss fundamentals and navigating market volatility with confidence. I’ll leave a link to register for the free event in the show notes section.
